title: Writing - Part 1: The Role of Advertising
description: Your class has attended a seminar on the influence of advertising on modern society.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the points in your notes. You should explain which point is more significant, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Advertising creates jobs and fuels the economy.
It helps people make informed choices about what to buy.
It makes people feel they need things they can't afford.

structure.introduction: Discuss the omnipresence of advertising in the digital age.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse how advertising contributes to economic growth.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the negative impact of social pressure and materialism.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate which effect has a more profound influence on society.

content.modelAnswer:
In the modern era, advertising has become an inescapable feature of our daily lives, permeating every digital and physical space we occupy. While often criticised for its intrusive nature, it plays a complex role in shaping both our economy and our psychology. This essay will examine the impact of advertising on economic growth and the social pressure it generates.

From an economic perspective, advertising is a powerful engine for growth. By generating demand for products and services, it encourages competition and innovation among businesses. When a company advertises a new technology, it not only seeks to increase its own profit but also informs the market, often leading to lower prices and better quality for consumers in the long run. Furthermore, the advertising industry itself is a major employer, supporting thousands of creative and analytical jobs worldwide.

However, the psychological cost of this economic drive is significant. Advertising frequently operates by creating a sense of inadequacy in the consumer, suggesting that happiness or social status can only be achieved through purchase. This constant exposure to idealised lifestyles exerts immense social pressure, particularly on younger generations, to conform to certain trends. This can lead to a cycle of overconsumption and debt, as individuals strive to meet the unrealistic standards set by marketing campaigns.

In conclusion, while the economic benefits of advertising are undeniable for the prosperity of a nation, I believe the social pressure it creates is the more significant impact. The shift towards a purely materialistic definition of success has deeper, more lasting consequences for mental well-being and social cohesion than the mere fluctuation of market figures. We must learn to navigate these messages with a more critical eye.
